content.text: Resource limits are hard, kernel-enforced caps on the compute, memory, network, storage I/O, and wall-clock time that a sandboxed AI agent process is permitted to consume. Unlike access-control policies that decide what an agent is allowed to do, resource limits constrain how much it can do -- bounding runaway computation, preventing memory exhaustion that would crash co-resident workloads, and stopping infinite loops before they burn through infrastructure budgets.

In the context of agentic AI systems, resource limits occupy a distinct and essential role in the overall security architecture. A sandboxed agent that can call arbitrary tools, execute code, read files, or make network requests is inherently capable of consuming unbounded resources -- whether by design, by bug, or by adversarial manipulation. Resource limits are the mechanical backstop that makes every other containment guarantee reliable: even if an agent is compromised or misbehaves, the damage it can inflict on the surrounding system is bounded by the resource envelope it was given.

Agentic AI systems differ from traditional software in that the code they execute is dynamically determined -- often generated by a language model at runtime. An agent asked to analyze a dataset might write and run a Python script that allocates 40 GB of memory while scanning every row; an agent asked to check the web might spawn hundreds of HTTP requests in parallel; an agent given a malformed task might loop indefinitely. These failure modes are qualitatively different from fixed-function software because neither the developer nor the model reliably anticipates them in advance.

Beyond accidental failure, adversarial resource exhaustion is a real threat. Prompt injection can cause an agent to enter an infinite loop, perform denial-of-service attacks against internal services, or exfiltrate data slowly through legitimate-looking network calls that exceed the agent's authorized scope. Resource limits don't just protect infrastructure -- they limit the blast radius of security incidents, making resource consumption itself a security signal that can be monitored and alerted on.

Financial exposure is another concrete driver. A code-execution agent that can spawn GPU workloads or make API calls without a ceiling can incur thousands of dollars of cloud cost in minutes. Resource limits translate security requirements into infrastructure policy, ensuring that the economic worst case for any single agent invocation is bounded and predictable.
A compromised or misbehaving agent cannot consume more resources than its limit, protecting co-resident workloads and the host system regardless of what code runs inside the sandbox.
Per-invocation CPU and memory ceilings translate directly to maximum per-task cost, enabling accurate billing and budget enforcement.
CPU and memory limits stop both accidental resource exhaustion (infinite loops, memory leaks) and intentional resource abuse (adversarially injected workloads).
Kernel-level limits (cgroups, RLIMIT, OS scheduler) are enforced outside the agent process. The agent cannot read, modify, or bypass them regardless of what code runs inside.
Limit-approaching events (throttling, near-OOM signals) are detectable metrics that indicate anomalous agent behavior before a full violation occurs.
Wall-clock timeouts ensure that a stuck agent is forcibly terminated rather than holding resources indefinitely -- critical for multi-tenant platforms where fairness matters.
Documented resource envelopes help satisfy AI governance requirements that mandate containment controls on autonomous systems with tool access.

Limits set too tight will kill valid workloads. A data analysis agent that legitimately needs 2 GB for a large dataset will fail if limited to 512 MB. Calibrating limits requires profiling real task distributions.

CPU limits throttle (slow down) rather than kill. A CPU-capped agent may take 10x longer than expected, causing downstream timeouts. Memory limits cause immediate OOM kills, which are harder to handle gracefully in application code.

Spawning a container or cgroup-constrained subprocess for each agent invocation adds latency (50-500ms for containers, 1-5ms for process-level cgroups). High-throughput systems need warm pool strategies.

CPU quota, CPU shares, memory limit, memory swap limit, pids limit, network bandwidth, disk IOPS, and wall-clock timeout all interact. Misconfiguring one can negate another -- unlimited swap defeats a memory limit.
Not a substitute for other controls:

Resource limits say nothing about what data the agent accesses or what external calls it makes. They must be combined with network egress controls, filesystem isolation, and capability restrictions to form a complete sandbox.

Resource limits for sandboxed processes are enforced at the Linux kernel level through control groups (cgroups). The kernel organizes processes into a hierarchy of groups, each with independent limits on CPU, memory, I/O, and network. When an agent process or container is created, it is placed into a cgroup with pre-configured limits. The kernel enforces these limits transparently -- the agent process never sees them and cannot influence them.

CPU limits in cgroups v2 use a quota/period model. The quota is the maximum CPU time (in microseconds) the cgroup may consume in each period. A cgroup with a 50ms quota in a 100ms period gets exactly 0.5 CPU cores worth of time. The effective CPU allocation follows:

The kernel tracks per-period CPU usage and throttles the cgroup -- suspending it -- when the quota is exhausted. Throttling is non-destructive: the process resumes at the start of the next period. However, if the agent is waiting on I/O during a throttled period, the elapsed wall-clock time still counts against downstream timeouts.
Memory limits and the OOM killer

Memory limits specify the maximum resident set size (RSS) the cgroup may hold. When an allocation would exceed the limit, the kernel's Out-Of-Memory (OOM) killer selects and terminates a process in the cgroup. To make memory limits effective, swap must also be disabled. In cgroups v2 this is expressed as:
to disable swap entirely. Without this, a process can exhaust physical memory and continue running on swap, violating the spirit of the limit.
Wall-clock timeouts are enforced by the sandbox orchestrator, not cgroups. The parent process starts a timer when the agent launches. On expiry it sends
(forced termination). This catches scenarios where the agent is CPU-idle (waiting on network) but stuck in an infinite polling loop -- CPU limits alone would not terminate it.
Network bandwidth is limited using Linux Traffic Control (
) with token bucket filters, or Docker's built-in bandwidth options. Disk I/O limits are set via the cgroup
controller, capping read/write IOPS and throughput. In practice, mounting agent workspaces as
) is the most common way to bound both storage and I/O simultaneously.

The diagram below shows how an AI agent's resource requests flow through the resource enforcer layer, where each dimension is checked against its configured quota. Requests within limits proceed; violations result in throttling, OOM termination, or SIGKILL depending on the resource type.

attrs.alt: Resource limits enforcement architecture showing CPU, memory, network, I/O, and time quotas applied to AI agent processes

content.text: Any agent that can execute arbitrary code, spawn subprocesses, or make network requests on behalf of a user.
Multi-tenant platforms where one user's agent must not affect another user's agent or the platform's shared infrastructure.
Agents processing untrusted content (user uploads, web pages, email) that may contain adversarial instructions designed to exhaust resources.
Production deployments where cost accountability matters -- limits enforce the economic contract with users or internal teams.
Simple text agents: 0.25 CPU cores, 128 MB memory, 30-second timeout. No code execution needed.
Code execution agents: 0.5-1.0 CPU cores, 256-512 MB memory, 60-second timeout, network isolated.
Data analysis agents: 1-2 CPU cores, 1-4 GB memory, 300-second timeout, disk I/O capped at 50 MB/s.
Web research agents: 0.5 CPU cores, 512 MB memory, 120-second timeout, network limited to allowlisted domains.
ML inference agents that run local model inference need 4-16 GB memory and GPU access -- limits must reflect these requirements, not be copied from lighter agent profiles.
Long-running background agents (hours-long tasks) need wall-clock timeout configured at the task level, not a global per-invocation timeout.
Profile existing agent tasks to measure actual CPU, memory, and duration distributions before setting limits. Limits set without profiling are guesses.
Choose a container runtime (Docker, containerd, gVisor) or cgroup-constrained subprocess as your enforcement mechanism. Containers are preferred for multi-tenant isolation.
Set memory and memory-swap to the same value to disable swap. This prevents memory-limited agents from silently exceeding their limit via swap.
Set CPU quota to limit concurrent CPU use. Add CPU shares (weight) so agents compete fairly when running concurrently.
Implement a wall-clock timeout in the orchestrator using a watchdog thread or async deadline. Send SIGTERM first (5s grace), then SIGKILL.
Mount the agent workspace as tmpfs with a size limit. This bounds storage and eliminates slow disk I/O from shared storage backends.
Set pids.max in cgroups to prevent fork bombs from exhausting the host PID namespace.
Log resource usage metrics (peak memory, CPU time consumed, wall-clock elapsed) for every invocation to drive limit calibration over time.

# sandbox_runner.py -- Run an agent subprocess with OS-level resource limits
import subprocess
import threading
import resource
import time
import signal
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ResourceLimits:
    """Per-invocation resource constraints for agent subprocesses."""
    cpu_seconds: float = 30.0            # RLIMIT_CPU: hard CPU-time cap
    max_memory_bytes: int = 256 * 1024 * 1024   # 256 MB virtual address space
    max_open_files: int = 64
    max_processes: int = 16
    wall_clock_timeout: float = 60.0    # seconds before SIGKILL

def apply_limits(limits: ResourceLimits) -> None:
    """Called in the child process after fork, before exec."""
    # CPU time limit (kernel sends SIGKILL when exceeded)
    resource.setrlimit(
        resource.RLIMIT_CPU,
        (int(limits.cpu_seconds), int(limits.cpu_seconds)),
    )
    # Virtual address space limit (covers heap + stack)
    resource.setrlimit(
        resource.RLIMIT_AS,
        (limits.max_memory_bytes, limits.max_memory_bytes),
    )
    # Open file descriptors
    resource.setrlimit(
        resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE,
        (limits.max_open_files, limits.max_open_files),
    )
    # Spawnable child processes (fork bomb protection)
    resource.setrlimit(
        resource.RLIMIT_NPROC,
        (limits.max_processes, limits.max_processes),
    )
    # No core dumps from sandboxed agents
    resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_CORE, (0, 0))

def run_agent_sandboxed(
    cmd: list[str],
    limits: Optional[ResourceLimits] = None,
    env: Optional[dict] = None,
) -> dict:
    """Execute cmd with resource limits. Returns result dict."""
    limits = limits or ResourceLimits()
    start = time.monotonic()

    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        cmd,
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
        env=env or {},         # Clean env -- no inherited secrets
        preexec_fn=lambda: apply_limits(limits),
        close_fds=True,
    )

    killed_by_timeout = threading.Event()

    def watchdog():
        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=limits.wall_clock_timeout)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            killed_by_timeout.set()
            proc.send_signal(signal.SIGTERM)
            try:
                proc.wait(timeout=5)
            except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
                proc.kill()

    watcher = threading.Thread(target=watchdog, daemon=True)
    watcher.start()
    stdout, stderr = proc.communicate()
    wall_clock = time.monotonic() - start

    return {
        "returncode": proc.returncode,
        "stdout": stdout.decode(errors="replace"),
        "stderr": stderr.decode(errors="replace"),
        "wall_clock_seconds": wall_clock,
        "timed_out": killed_by_timeout.is_set(),
        "oom_killed": proc.returncode == -signal.SIGKILL
                      and not killed_by_timeout.is_set(),
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    limits = ResourceLimits(
        cpu_seconds=10,
        max_memory_bytes=128 * 1024 * 1024,
        wall_clock_timeout=15,
    )
    result = run_agent_sandboxed(
        ["python3", "-c", "print(sum(range(1_000_000)))"],
        limits=limits,
    )
    print(f"Exit: {result['returncode']}, time: {result['wall_clock_seconds']:.2f}s")
    print(f"Output: {result['stdout'].strip()}")
Docker container with hard resource limits

# docker_sandbox.py -- Launch agent in Docker container with hard limits
import subprocess
import time
import tempfile
import os

def run_agent_in_docker(
    code: str,
    image: str = "python:3.12-slim",
    cpu_quota: float = 0.5,      # cores
    memory_mb: int = 256,
    timeout_seconds: int = 60,
    pids_limit: int = 32,
) -> dict:
    """
    Run agent code in a Docker container with strict resource limits.
    Returns dict with stdout, stderr, exit_code, timed_out.
    """
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
        suffix=".py", mode="w", delete=False
    ) as f:
        f.write(code)
        script_path = f.name

    try:
        cmd = [
            "docker", "run",
            "--rm",
            "--network", "none",              # No network access
            "--read-only",                     # Read-only root filesystem
            "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=64m",        # 64 MB writable tmpfs
            f"--memory={memory_mb}m",          # Memory hard limit
            f"--memory-swap={memory_mb}m",     # Same as memory = no swap
            f"--cpus={cpu_quota}",             # CPU quota in cores
            f"--pids-limit={pids_limit}",      # Fork bomb protection
            "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
            "--cap-drop", "ALL",               # Drop all Linux capabilities
            "--user", "65534",                 # Run as nobody (non-root)
            "-v", f"{script_path}:/agent/script.py:ro",
            "-w", "/tmp",
            image,
            "python3", "/agent/script.py",
        ]

        start = time.monotonic()
        proc = subprocess.run(
            cmd,
            capture_output=True,
            timeout=timeout_seconds,
            text=True,
        )
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        return {
            "exit_code": proc.returncode,
            "stdout": proc.stdout,
            "stderr": proc.stderr,
            "elapsed_seconds": elapsed,
            "timed_out": False,
        }
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        return {
            "exit_code": -1,
            "stdout": "",
            "stderr": "Container killed: wall-clock timeout exceeded",
            "elapsed_seconds": timeout_seconds,
            "timed_out": True,
        }
    finally:
        os.unlink(script_path)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Test: should OOM-kill (allocates 1 GB in a 64 MB container)
    oom_test = """
buf = []
for i in range(1000):
    buf.append(b'x' * 1024 * 1024)
print(f'allocated {len(buf)} MB')
"""
    result = run_agent_in_docker(oom_test, memory_mb=64, timeout_seconds=10)
    print(f"Exit: {result['exit_code']}")
    print(f"Stderr: {result['stderr'][:200]}")
Kubernetes Job manifest with resource limits

A developer tools company offers a cloud IDE with an integrated AI coding assistant that can write and execute Python code on behalf of users. Each code execution is a potential unbounded workload: users ask the agent to train small ML models, scrape web data, or process files. The platform runs dozens of concurrent user sessions sharing a fixed node pool.
Their resource limit architecture per agent invocation: 0.5 CPU cores (
), pids capped at 64, and a 90-second wall-clock timeout.

They monitor containers at 1-second intervals using Docker stats and emit Prometheus metrics. When a container's memory usage exceeds 80% of its limit, an alert fires. After deploying limits, their on-call incident rate from agent-caused host resource exhaustion dropped to zero. The most common limit hit in production turned out to be the wall-clock timeout -- triggered when users asked the agent to make HTTP requests to slow external endpoints. This was addressed with a dedicated error message explaining that network access is disabled inside the code sandbox.
Setting memory limit but not swap limit

# WRONG: memory limit set, but swap is still unlimited
# The agent can exceed 256 MB by using swap -- the limit is effectively bypassed
cmd_wrong = [
    "docker", "run", "--memory=256m",  # Only RSS limited
    "my-agent", "python3", "run.py",
]

# RIGHT: set memory-swap equal to memory to disable swap
cmd_right = [
    "docker", "run",
    "--memory=256m",
    "--memory-swap=256m",  # Same value = no swap at all
    "my-agent", "python3", "run.py",
]

# WRONG: application-level "self-monitoring" instead of OS enforcement
class Agent:
    MAX_MB = 256
    def run(self, task: str) -> str:
        import psutil, os
        if psutil.Process(os.getpid()).memory_info().rss > self.MAX_MB * 1024**2:
            raise MemoryError("self-imposed limit")
        # Agent can disable this check via adversarial code injection
        return self._execute(task)

# RIGHT: enforce at the kernel level, outside the agent's control
# (use Docker/cgroups, not application code)
Using cooperative timeout instead of process kill
Without a PID limit, a single container can exhaust the host's PID namespace with a fork bomb, even if CPU and memory are limited. Always include
in cgroup configuration. A limit of 64-128 is sufficient for nearly all legitimate agent workloads.
Run representative workloads in staging and measure P95 CPU seconds, peak memory, and wall-clock time. Set limits at 2-3x P95 to avoid legitimate failures while still bounding runaway cases.
Match --memory-swap to --memory in Docker, or set memory.swap.max=0 in cgroups v2. Memory limits without swap limits are partially ineffective.
Apply limits at OS (RLIMIT), container runtime (Docker/cgroup), and orchestrator (Kubernetes) layers simultaneously. Defense in depth applies to resource controls too.
Give the agent a 5-second grace period to flush logs and clean up before forcible termination. Hard kills without SIGTERM lose diagnostic information.
Set pids.max or --pids-limit on every container. Without it, a fork bomb exhausts the host PID namespace regardless of CPU and memory limits.
Mount the container filesystem read-only and provide only a size-capped tmpfs for writable scratch. This bounds storage and prevents persistent state outside the workspace.
Emit metrics when agents use more than 80% of memory limit or 90% of wall-clock timeout. These signals identify tasks needing limit recalibration and detect anomalous behavior before a hard kill.
Different agent task types have different resource needs. Match limits to task type rather than applying one universal profile that is either too tight or too loose for most tasks.

The runtime overhead of cgroup accounting is negligible -- it is performed by the kernel scheduler as part of normal scheduling work. The main performance cost is container startup latency for per-invocation sandboxing.
Good for quick local task execution
Use warm pool to reduce to <5ms effective latency
Best for batch/async; too slow for interactive tasks
5-20% CPU overhead; best for hostile untrusted code

For interactive use cases, pre-allocate a pool of running containers with limits already applied. When a task arrives, assign it a pool slot rather than starting a new container. After the task completes, reset the container state (terminate the process, clear tmpfs, reset file descriptors) and return it to the pool. This reduces effective latency from 100-500ms to under 5ms at the cost of idle container overhead.
The de facto standard for containerized agent sandboxing. Flags --memory, --cpus, --pids-limit, --network=none, and --read-only cover the essential resource limit axes in a single command.
ResourceQuota sets namespace-level aggregate caps; LimitRange sets per-Pod defaults and maxima. Together they enforce multi-agent resource fairness at the cluster level.
A user-space kernel that intercepts system calls, providing stronger isolation than standard Linux namespaces. Supported as a Kubernetes RuntimeClass (runsc). Adds 5-20% CPU overhead.
A lightweight VMM providing hardware-level isolation with sub-150ms startup. Used by AWS Lambda and Fargate. Ideal for agent sandboxes requiring near-VM isolation without full VM cost.
Standard library module (Unix only) for setting POSIX resource limits in child processes via preexec_fn. Covers RLIMIT_CPU, RLIMIT_AS, RLIMIT_NOFILE, RLIMIT_NPROC, RLIMIT_CORE.
A managed cloud sandbox service purpose-built for AI code execution. Provides pre-configured resource-limited sandboxes with a Python SDK that handles container lifecycle automatically.
cAdvisor exports per-container cgroup metrics (cpu_usage, memory_usage, network_transmit_bytes) to Prometheus. These metrics power dashboards and near-limit alerts.
LLM API cost and quota control

Think of resource limits as a prepaid phone plan for your agent. When you spin up the agent, you load it with a fixed budget: N CPU-seconds, M megabytes of memory, K seconds of real time, and X bytes of storage. The agent spends from these budgets as it works. When a budget runs out, the agent is stopped -- just as a phone stops making calls when its minutes are exhausted -- regardless of what it was doing.

The critical insight is that the budget is managed by the carrier (the kernel and container runtime), not by the phone (the agent process). The agent cannot give itself more minutes, cannot call the carrier to request an extension, and cannot see the mechanism enforcing the limit. This is the fundamental security property: limits are externally and unconditionally enforced. No matter how clever or adversarially manipulated the agent's code is, it cannot exceed the resource envelope it was placed in.

If the agent process can escalate to root (via setuid binary or kernel exploit), it can modify its own cgroup limits. Mitigation: run agents as non-root, drop all Linux capabilities with --cap-drop ALL, and apply seccomp profiles that block capability manipulation syscalls.

An agent that slowly allocates memory just below the limit never triggers OOM but holds resources indefinitely. Mitigation: enforce wall-clock timeouts alongside memory limits. No task should hold resources longer than its configured maximum duration.

An agent with write access to a shared volume can exhaust disk space outside its container's tmpfs limit. Mitigation: mount only task-specific tmpfs; never give agents write access to shared persistent storage.

Many containers near their limits can saturate host network or disk I/O, degrading other tenants. Mitigation: apply node-level I/O and network policies via Kubernetes, and use resource-aware scheduling to spread heavy agents across nodes.

OOM kills produce exit code -9 (SIGKILL) with no error output from the process itself. Implement orchestrator logic to detect returncode -9 and classify it as OOM for user-facing messages and metrics.

CPU throttling does not produce an error -- the process just runs slowly. A task that takes 10x longer than expected due to CPU throttling may succeed but trigger downstream timeouts. Monitor throttled_time_us from cgroup CPU stats to detect this.
Resource limits are kernel-enforced, not cooperative -- the agent process cannot read, modify, or bypass them, providing a reliable security boundary regardless of what code runs inside the sandbox.
Always set memory-swap equal to memory to disable swap; a memory limit without a matching swap limit is partially ineffective and gives a false sense of security.
Enforce limits at the OS (RLIMIT), container (cgroup), and orchestrator (Kubernetes) layers simultaneously. Defense in depth applies to resource controls.
Wall-clock timeouts complement CPU limits: a CPU-throttled agent waiting on network never consumes CPU but still holds resources indefinitely without a timeout.
PID limits are essential -- without them a fork bomb can exhaust the host PID namespace even when CPU and memory are limited.
Profile real agent workloads before setting limits. Target 2-3x the P95 observed peak to avoid killing legitimate tasks while still bounding runaway cases.
